coredata.dc:title: One-pot solvothermal synthesis of silane-functionalized carbon nanodots as compatibilizers for the immiscible TPU/MVQ blends
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Naiqun
Wang, Hanbin
Zhang, Liqun
Yue, Dongmei
Tian, Ming
coredata.dc:description: Herein, silane-functionalized carbon nanodots (Si-CDs) with different sizes and surface grafting density were successfully fabricated by a facile and cost-effective one-pot solvothermal treatment and incorporated for the first time as novel compatibilizers into the thermoplastic polyurethane/methyl vinyl silicone rubber (TPU/MVQ) blends. As obtained fluorescent Si-CDs with a size of 2 nm and functionalized with maximum amido and silane chains, called Si-CDs-High, displayed better compatibilizing efficiency than other Si-CDs with large size and low grafting density. The average diameter of MVQ domain in the blend reduced sharply from 2.51 ± 1.03 μm to 0.91 ± 0.28 μm at a small content of 1 wt% of Si-CDs-High. As effective compatibilizers, Si-CDs-High were located at the interface and formed a stable interface layer between TPU and MVQ phases and the thickness of interface was quantitatively identified by using Atomic Force Microscope. The superior compatibilizing efficiency of Si-CDs-High can be attributed that such Si-CDs with sufficient chains and groups can not only prevent coalescence of polymer domains by rigid nanoparticle core but also enhance interfacial adhesion due to the molecular entanglements and hydrogen bonding. Furthermore, the polymer blends with Si-CDs-High exhibited enhanced mechanical property, thermal stability and tunable fluorescent properties. This strategy compatibilized by fluorescent Si-CDs paves a new possibility for advanced blend nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Compatibilization
Immiscible polymer blends
Interface
Fluorescent property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis procedure for the silane-functionalized carbon nanodots
Compatibilization of Si-CDs for TPU/MVQ blends
Effects of Si-CDs particle size on the morphologies of the blends
Influence of grafting density of Si-CDs on the morphologies of the blends
Effects of Si-CDs loading on the morphologies and properties of the blends
Compatibilizing mechanism of Si-CDs for TPU/MVQ blends

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Organic–Inorganic Composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) size distribution of Si-CDs-High, (c) FT-IR spectra of Si-CDs-High and KH792, (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of Si-CDs-High recorded from 320 nm to 460 nm in ethanol solution.
SEM images of TPU/MVQ (70/30) blends with fixed contents (3 wt%) of (a) Si-CDs-High, (b) Si-CDs-Mid, (c) Si-CDs-Low.
SEM images of TPU/MVQ (70/30) blends illustrating the change of MVQ domain with various Si-CDs-High loading (wt%) (a) 0, (b) 1, (c) 3, (b) 5.
Average size of MVQ domains measured by SEM with various Si-CDs-High loading.
TEM images of TPU/MVQ (70/30) blends demonstrating interfacial encapsulation with various Si-CDs-High loadings (wt%):(a) 0, (b) 1, (c) 3, (d) 5.

Height, adhesion and logDMT Modulus images, the corresponding logDMT Modulus-width curve of TPU/MVQ (70/30) blends with the addition of various Si-CDs-High loadings (wt%) by AFM: (a1-a4) 0, (b1-b4) 1, (c1-c4) 3, (d1-d4) 5.
(a) Tan δ and (b) Storage modulus of TPU/MVQ (70/30) blends with various Si-CDs-High concentrations (wt%).
(a) Stress strain analysis and (b) FT-IR spectra for T70M30 blends with different concentration of Si-CDs-High.
Optical photograph of the T70M30 blends with various loading of Si-CDs-High under visible (left) and UV light (right).
Schematic of the preparation of Si-CDs and the compatibilizing mechanism of Si-CDs in TPU/MVQ blends.
Values of interfacial tensions and wetting coefficients calculated at 180℃.

head.ce:title: One-pot solvothermal synthesis of silane-functionalized carbon nanodots as compatibilizers for the immiscible TPU/MVQ blends

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Beijing City on Preparation and Processing of Novel Polymer Materials, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Beijing City on Preparation and Processing of Novel Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Beijing City on Preparation and Processing of Novel Polymer Materials, Beijing 100029, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, silane-functionalized carbon nanodots (Si-CDs) with different sizes and surface grafting density were successfully fabricated by a facile and cost-effective one-pot solvothermal treatment and incorporated for the first time as novel compatibilizers into the thermoplastic polyurethane/methyl vinyl silicone rubber (TPU/MVQ) blends. As obtained fluorescent Si-CDs with a size of 2 nm and functionalized with maximum amido and silane chains, called Si-CDs-High, displayed better compatibilizing efficiency than other Si-CDs with large size and low grafting density. The average diameter of MVQ domain in the blend reduced sharply from 2.51 ± 1.03 μm to 0.91 ± 0.28 μm at a small content of 1 wt% of Si-CDs-High. As effective compatibilizers, Si-CDs-High were located at the interface and formed a stable interface layer between TPU and MVQ phases and the thickness of interface was quantitatively identified by using Atomic Force Microscope. The superior compatibilizing efficiency of Si-CDs-High can be attributed that such Si-CDs with sufficient chains and groups can not only prevent coalescence of polymer domains by rigid nanoparticle core but also enhance interfacial adhesion due to the molecular entanglements and hydrogen bonding. Furthermore, the polymer blends with Si-CDs-High exhibited enhanced mechanical property, thermal stability and tunable fluorescent properties. This strategy compatibilized by fluorescent Si-CDs paves a new possibility for advanced blend nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer blending by melt mixing is a cost-effective and versatile strategy to develop novel materials combining the desirable properties of the both components [1–3]. The intrinsic thermodynamically immiscibility of most polymer pairs forces us to select appropriate compatibilizers for polymer blends to enhance the interactions between two phases and acquire high performance blends with favorable micromorphology and physical mechanical properties [4–6].

The classical approach for compatibilization is to add reactive components such as in-situ formed copolymers [7–9] or premade graft/block copolymer [10–12] into blends. Owing to be likely located at the interface, such “compatibilizers” can decrease interfacial tension, suppress particles coalescence effectively and eventually reduce the domain size and promote phase dispersion. Nevertheless, copolymers are prone to break away from the interface and convert into micelles in one phase due to the inherent flexibility, leading to the reduction of the compatibilizing efficiency [13]. The inevitable migration of copolymer makes it crucial to pursue novel types of compatibilizers and to increase the interfacial-localization stability of copolymers between polymers.

Inorganic nanoparticles [14–16] have been paid considerable attention to improve the compatibilization of the immiscible polymer blends because of their appropriate scale and high specific area. The inherent rigidity of the nanofillers such as nanosilica (SiO2) [17,18], polysilsesquioxane (POSS) [19–21], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [22,23], carbon black [24,25] and clay [26] enhances the immobilization of the interface between both polymers against high shear. For instance, Li and co-workers successfully improved compatibility of the poly( vinylidene fluoride)/poly(L-lactide) (PVDF/PLLA) blends with reactive SiO2 or CNTs with long poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) chains by melt reactive blending and the blends achieved a much better phase morphology through nanoparticles with pregrafted PMMA tails entangling PVDF molecular chains in the PVDF phase and the in situ grafted PLLA chains embedded in the PLLA phase [27,28]. Zheng and co-workers indicated that grafting of copolymers onto graphene nanoparticle had a potential in facilitating the compatibility of polystyrene/poly(methyl methacrylate) (PS/PMMA) blend through enhancing their interfacial adhesion, and the electrical conductivity of the blends get improved due to the inherent structure of graphene [29]. It’s noticed that pristine nanoparticles with no surface modification show poor interfacial adhesion and urge the need for surface functionalization to generate interactions with the corresponding polymer constituents. Nanoparticles functionalized with reactive groups or molecular chains not only prevent coalescence of polymer domains by rigid nanoparticle core but also enhance interfacial adhesion due to the molecular entanglements, which is sufficient at the molecular level without deteriorating mechanical properties. However, most of the modification strategies are complex, multistep and hard to be realized in large scale. Therefore, it is a challenge to find an easy synthetic method to obtain functional nanoparticles covered with abundant functional groups or molecular chains.

Carbon nanodots (CDs), a novel carbon nano-materials, as typical near-spherical structures with particle sizes generally less than 10 nm, are gaining widely attention in various fields due to their excellent properties, such as low toxicity, high photoluminescence (PL) emission, outstanding biocompatibility, eco-friendly synthesis [30–33]. The structure of CDs can be classified into carbon core and surface state. The functional groups such as hydroxyl, amido, carboxyl and long molecular chains on the surface of CDs can be designed according to the selection of wide materials sources [34,35]. Recent reports have demonstrated that CDs can act as oxidizing agents, reducing agents, radical scavengers and cross-linkers due to the diverse groups and unique structure [36–38].

In this work, inspired by the fantastic characteristics of CDs, we fabricated a series of silane functionalized CDs (Si-CDs) with different sizes and surface grafting density via one-pot solvothermal method. As-synthesized Si-CDs were used as new compatibilizers, for the first time, to promote the compatibilization of thermoplastic polyurethane/methyl vinyl silicone rubber (TPU/MVQ) blends. It is found that the Si-CDs with the surface of amino, and long silane coupling agent chains could produce intense interaction between the TPU and MVQ phases via molecular entanglements and hydrogen bonding. Meanwhile, such polymer blends with Si-CDs exhibit improved mechanical property and fluorescent properties derived from the inherent structure of Si-CDs. Hence, such Si-CDs prepared by one-step approach might be of importance in compatibilizing immiscible polymer blends and could even open a possibility for industrial purposes.

N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (KH792) was supplied by Energy-chemical Co. Ltd. Critic acid (CA, 98%) was fabricated by JK Co. Ltd. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), anhydrous alcohol and other solvents were from Beijing Chemical Company, China. All chemicals were used as received without purification. Commercially thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU, Trademark: 1185A) was manufactured by BASF Polyurethane Specialties (china) Co Ltd, made in china. Methyl vinyl silicone containing 0.07% vinyl groups (MVQ, Trademark: 110-1S) was purchased from Dong Jue Silicone (Nanjing) CO, LTD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis procedure for the silane-functionalized carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: Si-CDs were synthesized by solvothermal treatment according to previously published method with a little change.39 Briefly, 10 ml KH792 were placed into a 100 ml Teflon liner followed by adding a certain amount of CA and heated at 180℃ in an oven for 6 h. The resulting products were precipitated by petroleum ether three times to remove unreacted chemical. The grafting density of Si-CDs could be tuned by changing the amount of CA. Thus, three typical Si-CDs with various grafting density were synthesized through a similar solvothermal treatment procedure by pyrolysis of 10ml KH792 and 0.5g, 0.75g or 1.0g CA, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S1-S2, the grafting density of Si-CDs decreased with the increasing of the amount of CA, and thus, the corresponding Si-CDs named as Si-CDs-High, Si-CDs-Mid, Si-CDs-Low, respectively (‘High’, ‘Mid’ and ‘Low’ referred to the grafting density of Si-CDs).

Si-CDs with larger inorganic core (LCDs) was also obtained. 0.5 g CA was firstly dissolved into 10 ml of DMF, followed by adding 10 ml of KH792 under mildly stirring. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon lined steel autoclave and heated for 6 h at 180 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the materials were precipitated similarly to the method demonstrated above.

The TPU/MVQ (70:30, w/w) blends filled with Si-CDs were fabricated by a two-step melt mixing procedure. In the first step, solutions of Si-CDs and MVQ were prepared separately in chloroform. As-prepared solutions were mixed for 4 h at room temperature with magnetic stirrer. The mixture was then drying for 24 h at room temperature followed by dried under vacuum for 12 h. These blends are denoted as MVQ/Si-CDs, which were in the second step mixed with TPU by melt blending directly in a HAAKE internal mixer (HAAKE Rheomix 600 OS; Thermo Fisher Scientific, U.S.A.) at 180 °C with a rotation speed of 80 rpm/min for 6 min. The resulted blends were quenched into liquid nitrogen. TPU/MVQ blends with various Si-CDs-High loading were coded as T70M30Cx (x corresponding to Si-CDs-High loading). For comparison, TPU/MVQ blends were mixed with 3 wt% commercial ethylene methyl acrylate (EMA) according to the aforementioned process.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was performed on a Bruker Tensor 27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out with an ESCALAB 250 XPS system (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA). The UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a Jasco J-810 spectropolarimeter at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were measured on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Si-CDs were carried out with a HT7700 instrument (Hitachi, Japan). The Si-CDs solution was dropped onto a copper grid which was coated with carbon film. The transmission electron microscopy JEOL JEM-2100 (Hitachi, Japan) was also used for TPU/MVQ blends. An S-4800 instrument (Hitachi) was used to take the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) photographs. The MVQ phase has been removed by etching in toluene solvent and the number average size of MVQ domains (dn) was estimated according to the Eq. (1). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by an atomic force microscope (MultiMode 8, Bruker Corporation, Germany). The samples for AFM were prepared by using a cryo-ultramicrotome (Leica EM UC7, Germany) at −130 °C before the observation. Dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) was recorded on a DMA 1 with SRAR system at N2 atmosphere. The tests were performed from −135 °C to 120 °C at a frequency of 10 Hz with amplitude of 10 μm and the scanning rate of 3℃/min. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the blends was carried out using TGA analyser (Perkine Elmer, USA) under N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min from 30 °C to 800 °C. The tensile testing sample films were prepared by hot pressing at 180 °C and 15 Mpa followed by quenching to room temperature. The test samples were punched out into dumbbell shapes and the tensile tests were carried out using a tensile testing machine (Instron 3365, USA) at ambient temperature with a speed of 500 mm min−1.
(1)
d n =
∑ i
N i
d i
∑ i
N i

Scheme 1 illustrated the overall synthetic process. Si-CDs were synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal method which is cost-effective, high-yielding and sustainable [39]. CA was used as carbon source and KH792 was used as a coordinating solvent and surface passivation agent to synthesize Si-CDs. Furthermore, three kinds of Si-CDs (Si-CDs-High, Si-CDs-Mid, Si-CDs-Low) with decreased silane coupling agent grafting density were fabricated by adding CA loadings and the detailed PL and water contact angles tests were shown in Fig. S1–S2. Si-CDs-High with the highest grafting density has a production yield of ~82%. The TEM image (Fig. 1 (a)) and size distribution (b) show that the spherical Si-CDs-High have an average size of 2.0 nm. LCDs with the average diameter of 5 nm could be obtained through the same synthetic routes using DMF as solvents (see Fig. S3 in the supporting Information).

The FT-IR spectrum and XPS spectra were conducted to obtain more details about the components and structures of Si-CDs-High. In Fig. 1(c), a typical signal peak at 1659 cm−1 assigned to CONR indicates the successful acylation reaction. The broad and pronounced absorption peaks at 3642–3136 cm−1 indicate the existence of large amounts of –OH and –NH2 groups. The strong stretching vibrations at 2932 cm−1 and 1465 cm−1 correspond to the CH bonds. The characteristic absorption at 1126 and 1029 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration peak of SiOSi and SiOC confirm that the methoxy groups of KH792 is hydrolyzed. The XPS spectra (Fig. S4) also show that the Si-CDs-High mainly contain C, N, O, Si elements and their atomic percentages are shown in Table S1. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S4(b)) exhibits distinctive peaks of CON, CN, and CC at 287.6, 285.7, and 284.7 eV, [36] respectively. The main peak at 284.7 eV can be attributed to the CC corresponding to the sp2 carbon. The small peak of 287.6eVand 285.7 eV are assigned to OCN and CN respectively. In case of Si 2p spectra, two main peaks are at 102.4eVand 101.7 eV. The N 1s spectra (Fig. S4(d)) show the characteristic peaks of a NH2 and C-N at about 399.8eVand 398.5 eV [40] respectively. As confirmed by FT-IR and XPS results, abundant functional groups (NH2 and the silane coupling agent molecular chains) are detected on the surface of the Si-CDs-High, which impart excellent water solubility without further chemical modification. Moreover, the existence of hydrophobic methoxyl groups on Si-CDs-High surfaces leads to high solubility in organic solvents.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Si-CDs-High (Fig. S5) shows a typical peak at approximately 360 nm, which is attributed to the graphite structure [36]. Under a UV lamp (365 nm), the Si-CDs-High alcohol solution shows a bright blue fluorescence. When excited in the range of 320–460 nm, the alcohol solution of Si-CDs-High exhibits a maximum efficiency excitation band located at 360 nm as shown in Fig. 1(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compatibilization of Si-CDs for TPU/MVQ blends
Effects of Si-CDs particle size on the morphologies of the blends
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of Si-CDs size on compatibility performance and understand the spatial distribution of phases within the TPU/MVQ (70/30, w/w) blends with Si-CDs (3 wt%), two kinds of Si-CDs (Si-CDs-High and LCDs) with different sizes were investigated for comparison. As shown in Fig. S6, the black domain and grey phase refer to MVQ and TPU respectively. The SEM images show the typical sea-island morphology in TPU/MVQ blends with a well-distributed of MVQ-domain, indicating the Si-CDs have good interaction with the matrix and increase the compatibility between TPU and MVQ phases. Compared with the blends containing 2 nm of Si-CDs-High and 5 nm of LCDs, the latter has a broad distribution of MVQ-domains, which is in agreement with a previous study. Kwon et al [41] demonstrated that the smaller particles exhibited an outstanding ability to reduce the domain size. Si-CDs-High with 2 nm size was utilized to further discuss the surface grafting density effects in the next part.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of grafting density of Si-CDs on the morphologies of the blends
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effects of silane coupling agent grafting density, 3 wt% of Si-CDs with various grafting density were added into the TPU/MVQ blends (70/30, w/w) by melt compounding. As shown in Fig. 2 , with the addition of Si-CDs-Low, Si-CDs-Mid, Si-CDs-High into TPU/MVQ blends, the MVQ domain decreases linearly from 1.74 μm ± 0.28 to 0.81 μm ± 0.23. The obvious reduction of MVQ domain size with Si-CDs-High in TPU matrix is attributed to more grafts on the surface of the CDs. Higher grafting density of Si-CDs-High enhances the interaction between TPU/MVQ matrix and improve the compatibility for the TPU/MVQ blend.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Si-CDs loading on the morphologies and properties of the blends
ce:section.ce:para: Four blends of TPU/MVQ (70/30, w/w) were prepared to get a further investigation on the effect of Si-CDs-High loading from 0 to 5 wt%. In the case of 1 wt% loading of the Si-CDs-High (Fig. 3 b), the nearly circular MVQ domains shift from 2.51 ± 1.03 μm to 0.91 ± 0.28 μm in TPU matrix. It should be noticed that, upon increasing the concentration of Si-CDs-High into blends gradually, a visible reduction in the domain size and a narrower size distribution are observed, as shown in Fig. 3. It indicates that, as a considerable efficient nanoparticle compatibilizer, Si-CDs-High enhances the interfacial adhesion and suppress the MVQ domain coalescence through bridging two phases. The statistical analysis of MVQ-domain sizes is shown in Fig. 4 , with the increase of Si-CDs-High loading from 1 to 5 wt% in the blends, the sizes of MVQ domain decrease linearly and the domain distribution gets more uniform. Upon the addition of 5 wt% Si-CDs-High, approximately 4 times reduction of domain diameter is exhibited due to the thick interfacial layer which will be characterized by TEM and AFM in the next part.

TEM and AFM was used to investigate the location of Si-CDs-High in the blends. To allow an overview of localization of Si-CDs-High in the blends, the TEM images (Fig. 5 ) were performed. Upon the addition of the Si-CDs-High, the interface of the TPU/MVQ blends exhibits a black ring, which is referred to Si-CDs-High. From Fig. 5(b–d), Si-CDs-High locate selectively at the TPU/MVQ interface and the thickness of the black ring is increased gradually with increasing Si-CDs-High loading, indicating the favorable interaction of Si-CDs-High with TPU and MVQ. It is noticed that we can’t distinguish clearly the structure of the single Si-CDs-High located at the interface since the size of Si-CDs-High is only 2 nm, which is too small to be observed. The increased interfacial layer surrounding the MVQ domain provides a direct evidence of Si-CDs-High. Interfacial nanoparticles also stabilize the blend morphology due to the capacity of inhibition coalescence.

To further evaluate the effect of Si-CDs-High on interphase thickness, AFM was chosen as an accurate characterization. The high resolution images of height, logDMT modulus and adhesion force are shown in Fig. 6 . TPU phase with low adhesion, high Young’s modulus is assigned to matrix area and the sphere-like structure of above images correspond to the MVQ domain due to the high adhesion and low Young’s modulus [42]. Thus, based on the change in height, adhesion force or logDMT modulus, AFM has been developed as an efficient, convenient, accurate method to characterize the thickness of the interface layer. The height-width curve, adhesion force-width curve and logDMT modulus-width curve can be directly obtained by AFM computer soft through drawing a straight line traversing from the matrix area, interface area and MVQ area in height, adhesion force or logDMT modulus images. Thus, the corresponding width of transition region on the curve represents the thickness of interphase. We get the final results of the interfacial thickness of TPU/MVQ blends with various Si-CDs-High loading according to logDMT modulus-width curve. The thickness of 73 nm, 104 nm, 133 nm, 220 nm correspond to 0 wt%, 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt% Si-CDs-High loading respectively. The statistical analysis results of interphase thickness demonstrate that with the combination of the rigid Si-CDs-High into TPU/MVQ blends, the nanoparticles could stable at the interphase to maintain the morphology against the severe processing temperature and shear field. Thus, the large loading of Si-CDs-High contribute to obtain larger interfacial area and the smaller domain size, which represent the better compatibilization between the MVQ dispersion phase and the TPU matrix.

Fig. 7 shows the thermodynamic measurement, which was employed to further explore the compatibility of Si-CDs-High to TPU/MVQ blends. The glass transition temperatures (Tg) of MVQ and TPU approach each other with increasing the concentration of the Si-CDs-High from 0 to 5 wt%, suggesting the ability of Si-CDs-High to promote the miscibility of the two phases. As shown in Fig. 7b, storage moduli of all blends with different loading of Si-CDs-High are found to be increased with the increase of the filler dosage. The main mechanical properties of TPU/MVQ blends with different amounts of Si-CDs-High are shown in Fig. 8 a and the details were listed in Table S2. It’s noted that the tensile stress of the blends with 3 wt% of Si-CDs-High is 27.4 MPa, which is more than the blends without Si-CDs-High compatibilizers. The improved mechanical properties can be attributed to the enhanced interfacial interaction. The interfacial nanoparticles could transfer stress under external force. However, with the addition of 5 wt% Si-CDs-High, the mechanical properties of TPU/MVQ blends decrease to 24.6Mpa due to the excessive thick Si-CDs-High interfacial layer. This indicates that larger amount of Si-CDs-High saturating the interface could reduce the transmission of strength between the two phases. Hence, the loading of Si-CDs-High would be kept in a reasonable range from 1 to 3 wt% based on micro-morphology and mechanical properties results. As shown in Fig. S7(a), the TGA curves of TPU/MVQ blends with various Si-CDs loadings varies little before 500℃ and the degradation starts at 287℃. The obvious distinction of TGA curve is at 500-700℃. Combining with DTG curves (Fig. S7b), three degradation processes of TPU/MVQ blends referred to TPU soft and hard segments and MVQ increase to higher temperatures with the addition of Si-CDs-High, as compared to those pristine blends. The remarkable change is the onset temperature of MVQ degradation, which is raised from 548℃ to 598 °C for the blends with 5 wt% Si-CDs-High. The introduction of Si-CDs improves the thermal stability of TPU/MVQ blends. For comparison, the TPU/MVQ blends mixed with 3 wt% EMA was also prepared and the SEM image was shown in Fig. S8. Compared with Si-CDs-high, the larger size and distribution of MVQ domain demonstrates that the compatibilization activity of Si-CDs-High is superior to that of EMA due to the rigid core of Si-CDs and better interaction between Si-CDs and the two phases.

We also note that with the addition of Si-CDs-High into TPU/MVQ blends, the blends exhibit blue fluorescent at 1 wt% and green fluorescent at 5 wt% compared with the blends without Si-CDs-High compatibilizer under a UV lamp, as shown in Fig. 9 . The unique fluorescence properties and the various fluorescence color of TPU/MVQ blends are attributed to the nature of the CDs. Therefore, the Si-CDs-High in blends are in favor of promoting the compatibilization between the dispersion phase and matrix and also give a novel and tunable fluorescence property to blends.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compatibilizing mechanism of Si-CDs for TPU/MVQ blends
ce:section.ce:para: Abundant NH2 functional groups on the surface of Si-CDs-High improve the interaction between TPU/MVQ blends through the formation of hydrogen bond between NH2 of Si-CDs-High and CO of TPU. FT-IR spectrum was used to characterize the form of hydrogen bonds between Si-CDs-High and TPU. Fig. 8(b) shows pure TPU/MVQ blend and its nanocomposites with the addition of Si-CDs-High. The peak at approximately 3350 cm−1 was observed in spectrum of pure TPU/MVQ blend referring to the hydrogen-bonds urethane NH groups, which was shifting to high wave numbers with the increase in the loading of Si-CDs-High. The gradually shifting peak means a reduced strength of hydrogen-bonded urethane NH groups of TPU [43], indicating that the NH/CO hydrogen bonds of TPU was disrupted due to the formation of novel hydrogen bonds between the CO groups in the Hard segment of pure TPU and the NH groups of Si-CDs-High. From the results above and as shown in scheme 1, as-prepared Si-CDs-High contains NH2 groups and silane coupling agent molecular chains on a rigid core, the former can form new hydrogen-bonds between a NH groups of Si-CDs-High and a CO group in the hard segment of TPU, improving the interaction between Si-CDs-High and TPU. In addition, the molecular entanglement between the silane coupling agent molecular of Si-CDs-High and MVQ chains can be formed because of the better compatibility. Si-CDs-High located at the interface between TPU and MVQ phases could generate chemical interaction with TPU and silane coupling agent molecular entanglement with MVQ simultaneously, suggesting that the rigid Si-CDs-High acts as a physical barrier to enhance interfacial stability, improve interfacial adhesion, prevent coalescence of MVQ chains and effectively promotes the compatibility of the TPU/MVQ blends.

The location of the Si-CDs-High (the Si-CDs-High is named as CDs in this part) particles is desirable to be predicted by thermodynamics. CDs can be estimated in two situations: in one of the two phases or located at the interface between the two polymers. It can be evaluated by comparing surface tension of the three components [44]. Here in, wetting coefficient ω (Eq. (2)) is used to estimate the position of the CDs.
(2)
ω =
γ
CDs - T P U
-
γ
CDs - M V Q
γ
TPU - M V Q
γ
CDs - T P U
,
γ
C D s - M V Q
and
γ
TPU - M V Q
denote the interfacial tension within the CDs-TPU interface, CDs-MVQ and TPU-MVQ respectively. When ω＞1, CDs are expected to be present in MVQ phase; when ω < −1, CDs are only found to be in the TPU matrix. While −1 < ω < 1, CDs would be concentrated at the TPU/MVQ interface.
The harmonic equation and geometric equation can be used to evaluate the interfacial tension from the surface tensions of each component.

The geometric equation:
(3)
γ 12
=
γ 1
+
γ 2
- 2
γ
1
d
γ
2
d
- 2
γ
1
p
γ
2
p

The harmonic equation:
(4)
γ 12
=
γ 1
+
γ 2
-
4 γ
1
d
γ
2
d
γ
1
d
+
γ
2
d
-
4 γ
1
p
γ
2
p
γ
1
p
+
γ
2
p

Here,
γ
d
and
γ
p
(
γ i
=
γ
i
d
+
γ
i
p
) represent for the dispersive and the polar contributions of the components. The surface tension values of TPU at room temperature are obtained from the literature [18]. As for MVQ and CDs particles, the grades of the surface tension are calculated by contact angle and listed in Table S3. Given the temperature dependent upon the surface tension, it is desirable to utilize temperature coefficient
d γ / d T
to evaluate γ at other temperatures. According to the previous literature, the temperature coefficient of polymers (TPU, MVQ) and nanoparticles are approximately −0.06 mJ/m2/K and −0.1 mJ/m2/K respectively [18]. As shown in Table 1 , the interfacial tensions of the TPU-CDs and MVQ-CDs are calculated to be 9.49 mN•m−1 and 0.32 mN•m−1 at 180℃. The calculated wetting coefficient is 0.83, implying that CDs particles should locate at the interface between the TPU and MVQ at the thermodynamic equilibrium, which is in agreement with the above TEM and AFM results.

We utilized Si-CDs as novel compatibilizers to promote the compatibilization of the immiscible TPU/MVQ blends via melt blending for the first time. The CDs with silane coupling agent molecular chains and NH2 groups were synthesized by a simple, cost-effective, one-pot solvothermal method. The silane coupling agent chain attached onto Si-CDs surface could entangle with MVQ chains and the amino groups on the surface of Si-CDs develop hydrogen bond with the CO of TPU. Si-CDs-High acted as effective compatibilizers located at the interface between the TPU and MVQ phases and stabilized the blend morphology though inhibiting MVQ domain coalescence. A systematic study referred to the size, silane grafting density, loading of CDs was performed. Si-CDs-High with a 2 nm rigid core and maximum graft density can improve the tensile strength of TPU/MVQ blend from 21.3Mpa to 27.4 Mpa at 3 wt% content compared with the pristine blend. Moreover, with the increase of Si-CDs loadings, the thermal stability of TPU, MVQ phases is enhanced gradually and especially in the degradation process of MVQ. The resultant CDs compatibilized TPU/MVQ blends also exhibit tunable fluorescence due to the nature of CDs. The present strategy might also be applied for various types of multiphase polymer materials though changing the type of CDs by adjusting carbon source and passivation agent which give a green, sustainable and universal design in immiscible polymer blending.

Naiqun Yao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Hanbin Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Liqun Zhang: Supervision. Ming Tian: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Dongmei Yue: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.147124.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
